content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION


July 12, 1995


John R. Harper
Law Offices of Harper & Burns
453 S. Glassell Street
Orange, California 92666

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-144

Dear Mr. Harper:

This is in response to your question regarding the valuation
and reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act (the
"Act")¹ of an honorary social membership provided to City of
Murrieta officials by the Bear Creek Golf Club.

Please note that the Commission's advice is prospective in
nature. Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate
any conduct which may have already taken place.

QUESTION

What is the value of an honorary membership provided to the
City of Murrieta for purposes of the Act's gift limits and for
purposes of the Act's disclosure and disqualification
requirements?

CONCLUSION

The value of the honorary country club membership is the fair
market value of the membership, which includes the initiation fee
of $1,000, plus the monthly fee of $50 for each month in which the
public official, or a member of his or her immediate family, uses
the club facilities.

_______________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-144
Page 2


FACTS

The City of Murrieta has received from Bear Creek Golf Club
an "Honorary Social Membership." There are no charges directly
associated with this membership, other than purchased meals,
merchandise and golf rounds. The membership is extended to the
city through the city manager and is to benefit the "City Family,"
including councilmembers.

There is no specific list of which city officials are
permitted to use the honorary membership in addition to city
councilmembers, but it has been used in the past by the police
chief, fire chief, city manager, assistant city manager, and as a
practical matter, all of the City of Murrieta's management staff.
There is no limit on the number of city officials using the
honorary membership at the same time.

The city officials' use of the honorary membership is not
restricted to official city business, although you stated that
this has been the actual practice.

The honorary membership allows city officials and their
immediate family members to use the club's restaurant facilities.
The use of golf facilities is limited to the city officials
themselves. The honorary membership does not specifically state
whether city officials may bring guests, although you stated that
probably a city official and a guest would be allowed to use the
facilities at their cost.

Although there is no directly comparable membership at Bear
Creek, the closest is a family social membership. This membership
is available upon payment of a $1,000 initiation fee and monthly
dues of $50. The membership extends club privileges to the member
and the member's immediate family.

Social members have no specific golfing privileges. When
they play, unless they are the guest of another member, they must
pay the unescorted guest fee. This is true of city-sponsored
players as well.

The city membership is terminable by either party, non-
assessable and non-equity. These characteristics are also true of
the social membership. The honorary membership is not
transferable.

ANALYSIS

Councilmembers and the other specified city officials, such
as the fire chief, police chief, city manager, and assistant
manager, of the City of Murrieta are considered public officials
with full disclosure obligations under the Act. (Sections 82048
and 87200.) As such, they must file annual statements of economic
interests and disclose any gifts received of $50 or more in value.

Our File No. A-95-144
Page 3


(Section 87207.) In addition, the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act may apply to gifts from any one source aggregating $280
or more. (Section 87103.)

Furthermore, the Act contains gift limits applicable to local
officials. Section 89501(b) provides that:

     (b) No local elected officeholder ... or
designated employee of a local government agency
shall accept any gifts, from any single source
which is in excess of [two hundred eighty dollars
($280)], in any calendar year, except reimbursement
for actual travel expenses and reasonable
subsistence in connection therewith.

                                          Section 89501(b).

Section 82028(a) of the Act defines a "gift" as:

     [A]ny payment to the extent that consideration
of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of
anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members
of the public without regard to official status.
Any person, other than a defendant in a criminal
action, who claims that a payment is not a gift by
reason of receipt of consideration has the burden
of proving that the consideration received is of
equal or greater value.

                                          (Emphasis added.)

     The City of Murrieta has been provided with an honorary
membership to the Bear Creek Golf Club for use by councilmembers
and other city officials. Under the Act, the honorary membership
constitutes a gift to the councilmembers and city officials who use
it.

     As a general rule, gifts are to be valued at fair market
value. (Section 82025.5.) Regulation 18946 provides guidance for
determining the value of gifts generally. To determine the value
of gift passes or honorary memberships to country clubs, we have
used Regulation 18946.1 to determine the value of the gift which is
being provided.² (Bergeson Advice Letter, No. A-92-300; and
Craigie Advice Letter, No. A-89-441.)

_________________
2 We have also applied Regulation 18944.1 to determine whether a
pass or membership provided to a city constitutes a gift to public
officials where the honorary membership was limited for use to
city officials and their immediate families. Under the facts
presented, guests of the officials could also use the honorary
membership. Therefore, Regulation 18944.1 would not apply.

Our File No. A-95-144
Page 4


Regulation 18946.1(a) attempts to value the benefit received
by a public official by measuring the official's actual use of the
membership for purposes of disclosure and the gift limits.
Regulation 18946.1(b) sets out a separate rule for valuing the
gifts for purposes of disqualification. This rule measures the
maximum reasonable benefit to be derived by the public official.
For example, in the Craigie Advice Letter, supra, for purpose of
disqualification, the value of the membership was the value based
on the actual use during the preceding 12 months, plus a monthly
fee of $30 per month for each of the months in the future that the
membership was valid. The membership was renewable annually. In
the Bergerson Advice Letter, supra, the maximum value of the
membership was based on the initiation fee, plus a monthly fee
during the 12-month period in which it was valid.

According to your facts, the honorary membership is available
for use by City of Murrieta officials and councilmembers for an
indefinite period of time, not on an annual basis or any other
specific time period. It appears akin to a lifetime membership
which, under these facts, should be valued under the general
valuation rule set forth in Regulation 18946.1 Regulation 18946(a)
provides that gifts shall be valued at fair market value as of the
date of receipt or promise. Thus, in determining the fair market
value of the honorary membership in question, it is reasonable to
look at the value of a comparable membership available to the
public. For example, in the Carpenter Advice Letter, No. 75-10-
145, we advised that guest memberships in a social or fraternal
organization would constitute a gift, and the value of the
membership would be the price an individual would have to pay in
order to join the organization.

Also according to your facts, the President of the Bear Creek
Golf Club stated in a memorandum to you that the most directly
comparable membership to the honorary membership is a "family
social membership." A family social membership is available upon
payment of a $1,000 initiation fee and monthly dues of $50. Like
the honorary membership, this membership extends privileges to the
member and the member's immediate family. Under the honorary
membership, as under a family social membership, city officials are
required to pay the unescorted guest fee for golf. Thus, in

_________________________

3 For example, it would be difficult, if not impossible, to
determine the maximum reasonable benefit to be derived by the gift
following the date of a decision for purposes of Regulation 18946.1(b).

4 "Fair market value" of a gift is the value that
the gift would command in the open market. (In re Hopkins (177) 3
FPPC Ops. 107.) Also, "fair market value" means the price at
which bona fide sales have been consummated for assets of like
type, quality, and quantity in a particular market at the time of
acquisition. (Blacks Law Dictionary (1979) Fifth Edition, West
Publishing Company.)

Our File No. A-95-144
Page 5


determining the fair market value of the honorary membership, it
seems appropriate to apply the family social membership fee since
it is the most comparable membership which is available to the
public.

Consequently, the value of the honorary membership described
in your letter, for all purposes, would be the initial fee of
$1,000 when an official initially uses the privilege, plus a
monthly fee of $50 for each month in which the public official or
a member of his or her immediate family, uses the club facilities.
As local elected officials or designated employees, the City of
Murrieta councilmembers and officials are prohibited from receiving
gifts from a single source in excess of $280 in a calendar year.5
They would be prohibited from accepting this membership at all
because its value exceeds $280.6

I trust this answers your question. If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 916/322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Luisa Menchaca
     Counsel, Legal Division




5    The gift limit applicable to local officials which was $1,000
per year in 1994, was reduced to $280 per year in 1995.

6    Please note, however, that an official may, within 30 days of
receipt or acceptance, reimburse the donor, or the donor's agent
or intermediary, for all or a portion of the gift. In such event,
the value of the gift is reduced by the amount of the
reimbursement, and the amount of any gift is reduced by the amount
of reimbursement. (Regulation 18943(a).)
